title: Predicting the Effect of Hardener Composition on the Mechanical and Fracture Properties of Epoxy Resins Using Molecular Modeling
abstract: Improving the toughness of brittle epoxy while keeping its high strength-to-weight ratio is challenging, as these two properties work against each other. Fracture processes are difficult to ascertain with experiments, as they occur at nanoscopic lengths and time scales and require higher efficiency than what can be attained with atomistic simulations. To overcome this challenge, we utilize a recently developed chemistry specific coarse-grained model to examine two different hardeners, diamine 4,4'-methylene-bis(cyclohexylamine) (PACM) and propylene oxide diamine (Jeffamine), to cure bisphenol A diglycidyl ether (DGEBA) at varying stoichiometries and understand how hardener composition influences the elasticity, yield strength, and fracture toughness of epoxy resins. The results indicate that PACM mainly contributes to the modulus and that long Jeffamine chains increase fracture toughness by making the epoxy ductile, whereas short Jeffamine chains significantly improve the yield strength. Longer Jeffamines also lead to larger voids and the formation of fibrils that carry a significant amount of stress and contribute to toughness. Interestingly, the Ashby plots reveal that epoxies with intermediate-length Jeffamine chains (D800 and D2000) outperform other systems, as the toughness enhancement from flexible Jeffamine chains and the stiffness due to PACM help to overcome the strength—toughness trade-off. Our modeling framework and findings establish a path toward resin design from predictive multiscale models with no empirical input and reveal new insights into the molecular failure mechanisms of epoxy resins.
results: Mechanical Properties. We first discuss the stress-strain responses to provide an overall understanding of the effect of PACM and Jeffamine on constitutive behavior. Mechanical testing was carried out for each of the five Jeffamine chains with five %PACM fractions, for a total of 25 compositions. In Figure 2a, selected stress-strain plots are shown for one Jeffamine length (D400) with varying %PACM; similarly, Figure 2b shows the same for a fixed PACM fraction (%PACM = 40%) and varied Jeffamine lengths. All the plots have similar features: a clear yield point  $(\sigma_v)$ , a small strain softening region, a post-yield stress plateau  $(\sigma_p)$ , and a strain hardening regime which ultimately leads to failure of the epoxy. The width (0.2– 0.8 strain) of the plateau region directly affects the fracture toughness as the chain drawing and void formation processes happen in this regime. This will be elaborated on in later sections. The effect of the PACM fraction on the stress-strain curve is apparent but not substantial (see Figure 2a). The yield stress  $(\sigma_{v})^{-}$  and plateau stress  $(\sigma_{p})$  increase slightly with increasing %PACM. Changing the Jeffamine chain length and, consequently, its fraction has a more profound effect on the stress–strain curves. The epoxies with D230, D400, and D800 fail at  $\epsilon=2$  and 3, but D2000 and D4000 show no failure until  $\epsilon=5$ . The failure strain is larger than the experimental values. This is expected since simulations rely on ideal, defect-free, small-scale specimens that exhibit a lesser degree of strain-localization than macroscale samples. The yield strength  $(\sigma_y)$  and ultimate strength  $(\sigma_{max})$  both go down with increasing Jeffamine chain length. However, the strain to failure and toughness increases, indicating that longer Jeffamine chains make the epoxies more ductile.
discussion: Failure Mechanism. In this section, we examine the nanoscale failure mechanisms. Previous atomistic studies have reported bond scission, void formation, and growth during the deformation of epoxies or similar network-like structures. For a comparative analysis, we consider two epoxy compositions, D230-40 and D4000-40, as representative examples of short and long Jeffamine chains, respectively. D230-40 is expected to be much stiffer than D4000-40 due to its shorter Jeffamine chain lengths. We first investigate the bond breaking as it is implemented in the simulations. Figure 4a shows the percentage of amine bonds broken during uniaxial stretching along with the type of the bond (either Jeffamine-DGEBA or PACM-DGEBA). Bond breaking strictly occurs at the amine connections (E_n − N_2, refer to Figure 1). For D230-40, the majority of bond scission happens between $\epsilon = 1−2$, where the stress reaches a maximum value, indicating a brittle behavior. However, in D4000-40, the amine bonds break slowly in a step-by-step manner, showing the stark difference in response created by the longer Jeffamine chains. Therefore, increasing the Jeffamine chain length as we go from D230 to D4000 changes the failure mode gradually from brittle to ductile. The brittle failure correlates to higher strength; as a result, D230-PACM or D400-PACM have comparatively high $E$ and $\sigma_y$ but worse $\epsilon_{\sigma_{max}}$ and fracture energy ($G_c$). In both epoxies, Jeffamine-DGEBA bonds break more than PACM-DGEBA, as 60% of the hardener is Jeffamine. At maximum stress, ∼2.9% of the total amine bonds have failed. Example animations of individual bond break events are provided as Movies S1 and S2.
conclusion: In this paper, we successfully implemented the chemistry specific CG-MD based framework and discussed the effect of using a mixed hardener on the various mechanical and structural properties of epoxy resins. The fracture energy is predicted from molecular simulations employing continuum scale theory. Moreover, we are able to find an optimal Jeffamine chain, D800, which has not been studied before or used commercially and which performs better than others. Our findings illustrate the competing effect of two hardeners, PACM and Jeffamine. The model predicts higher elastic modulus and yield strength for epoxies with higher PACM percentages. On the other hand, Jeffamine contributes to extensibility, especially longer Jeffamine chains. This leads to larger failure strain and thus higher fracture energy. Short Jeffamine chains promote higher yield strength arising from the uniformity of the networks. We dig deeper to understand the failure mechanisms from the molecular viewpoint. Bond scission and void formations were prominent in every epoxy composition. We find that the majority of the scissions happened during strain hardening in the system. Epoxies with longer Jeffamine chain show an increase in void size and number of voids at post-yield. We also discover that the change in mechanical properties depends on material properties as well as the network architecture of the cross-links. High fracture toughness is observed for longer Jeffamine chain epoxies. The computed $K_{Ic}$ value exhibited good agreement with recent experimental results, thus demonstrating the predictive power of the framework. We presented a comparison between strength and toughness using Ashby plots which reveals that the epoxies with D800 and D2000 perform better than others. This can be further extended to study disulfide based epoxy resins and hardeners.
notes: Used 'Conclusion' as the equivalent for 'Concluding Remarks'.
